metadata.title: RLHF Preference Data: Chosen/Rejected Pairs and Why Pairwise Wins
metadata.source: Bai et al. 2022 — Training a Helpful and Harmless Assistant with RLHF, Anthropic

props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · RLHF Preference Data: Chosen/Rejected Pairs and Why Pairwise Wins

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. A preference pair is one prompt with a chosen and a rejected response, labeled by a human annotator. The reward model trains on thousands of these triples to predict which response a human would pick. Pairwise beats absolute scoring because relative judgments are more reliable and need no shared calibration scale. Chosen and rejected must share the prompt, and the margin and rejected-response distribution shape what the model learns.

Pairwise beats absolute scoring because relative judgments are more reliable and need no shared calibration scale.
Chosen and rejected must share the prompt, and the margin and rejected-response distribution shape what the model learns.

beats.narration_text: Here is why RLHF Preference Data: Chosen/Rejected Pairs and Why Pairwise Wins belongs in your working knowledge. The systems, policies, and tools shaped by it affect decisions at your level — whether or not you understand the mechanism. Knowing the underlying logic gives you the frame; missing it means reasoning from output with no access to the inputs.

beats.narration_text: I get that a preference pair is one prompt with a chosen and a rejected response, and that the margin between them shapes reward-signal quality. I want to build a tiny dataset myself. Can you (1) write me three preference triples on one shared prompt, deliberately varying the margin from near-tie to obvious, (2) explain how the reward model would treat the near-tie versus the clear pair during training, and (3) show what breaks if I accidentally let chosen and rejected come from different prompts?

props.segment: RLHF Preference Data: Chosen/Rejected Pairs and Why Pairwise Wins
props.command: RLHF requires human preference data. The structure of that data determines what the reward model learns. The Anthropic HH dataset uses pairwise comparisons: chosen/rejected for the same prompt. That structure is why RLHF works at scale.

beats.narration_text: A preference pair is a triple: one prompt, one chosen response, one rejected response. The chosen response is what a human annotator preferred. The rejected response is what they did not prefer. The reward model trains on thousands of these triples — it learns to predict which response a human would choose. That trained signal becomes the reward function for reinforcement learning.

Pairwise beats absolute rating for two reasons. First: humans are better at relative judgment than absolute scoring. Saying 'A is better than B' is easier and more reliable than 'A scores 7.3 out of 10.' Second: pairwise comparison is scale-invariant — it does not require calibrating annotators to a shared absolute standard. The pair is self-contained. The only required judgment is: which one is better?

The data structure has consequences downstream. Chosen and rejected must share the same prompt — otherwise the model learns prompt differences, not quality differences. The margin between chosen and rejected matters: near-ties produce noisier reward signals than clear preferences. And the distribution of rejected responses shapes what the model learns to avoid. Garbage collected pairs produce a garbage reward model.

Your turn. Ask Claude: Generate five preference pairs for a helpful assistant benchmark. Each pair: a prompt, a chosen response that is genuinely more helpful, and a rejected response that sounds plausible but is subtly worse. Then explain the quality criterion you used. The quality criterion is the part you're actually training. Make it explicit. Kore, in for Humanitarians AI.
